title: Strings
description: Rust has two string types: owned String and borrowed &str. Master the conversions between them, character iteration, splitting/collecting, and format! to handle any string manipulation interview problem.

sections.title: String vs &str
sections.explanation: **String** is an owned, heap-allocated, growable UTF-8 string. **&str** is an immutable reference ("string slice") to UTF-8 data. String literals are `&str`. Convert with `.to_string()` or `String::from()` to go &str -> String, and `&s` or `.as_str()` to go String -> &str. In interviews, use String when you need to build or modify, &str for read-only parameters.

exercise.prompt: Create a &str "hello", convert it to a String. Create a String from "world" using String::from. Print both strings and their lengths.

validation.message: Both strings printed followed by their lengths (5 each)
Use .to_string() and String::from() for the two conversion styles

exercise.hint: Pass "world" to String::from(). Use .len() on s3 for the last line.

sections.title: Character iteration with chars()
sections.explanation: Rust strings are UTF-8 encoded, so you can't index them by byte position. Use **.chars()** to iterate over Unicode characters. For interview problems that need character-level access, collect into a `Vec<char>` first: `s.chars().collect::<Vec<char>>()`. This gives O(1) indexing.

exercise.prompt: Given the string "rust", iterate over its characters and print each on its own line. Then collect into a Vec<char> and print the character at index 2.

validation.message: Each character on its own line, then chars[2] = 's'
Use .chars() to iterate, .collect() to build Vec, and index with [2]

exercise.hint: Call .collect() on the chars iterator. Index 2 of "rust" is 's'.

sections.title: Split, join, and collect
sections.explanation: Use **.split()** to break a string by a delimiter — it returns an iterator of &str slices. Chain with `.collect::<Vec<&str>>()` to get a vector. Use `.join()` on a Vec/slice of strings to combine them back. This pattern appears in parsing problems constantly.

exercise.prompt: Split the string "one:two:three:four" by ':' and collect into a Vec. Print the Vec. Then join the parts with a space and print the result. Finally, print the number of parts.

validation.message: Split by ':', join with space, and count = 4
Use split(':') to separate, join(" ") to rejoin, and len() to count

exercise.hint: Join with a space: parts.join(" "). Use .len() to get the count.

sections.title: format! and string building
sections.explanation: Use **format!()** to build formatted strings (like println! but returns a String instead of printing). For building strings incrementally, use `String::new()` with `.push_str()` (append &str) or `.push()` (append char). This is faster than repeated concatenation with `+`.

exercise.prompt: Use format! to create a string "Result: 42 (true)". Then build a comma-separated string from numbers 1 to 5 using push_str. Print both.

validation.message: format! produces the message, push_str builds the comma-separated list
Use format! for the message and push_str with to_string() for building

exercise.hint: Use i.to_string() to convert the integer to a string before pushing.
